skill.name: pydicom
skill.description: Guidance and helper scripts for reading, converting, anonymizing, and inspecting DICOM medical imaging files with pydicom.
skill.summary: Work with DICOM metadata, pixel data, transfer syntaxes, and local helper scripts using pydicom.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, weak-crypto, and heuristic issues, but review found these are mainly false positives from Markdown examples, DICOM UID values, and documentation links. The confirmed risks are medium privacy risks because the scripts read, print, write, and partially anonymize medical imaging data that can contain PHI. No evidence found of malicious network calls, runtime shell execution, credential theft, obfuscation, or prompt injection.

medium_findings.title: Medical PHI can be extracted or printed by metadata tooling
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE. The metadata extractor intentionally reads patient, study, series, and image metadata, then prints it or writes it to a user-selected file. This is legitimate for a DICOM utility, but it can expose protected health information if used on real clinical data without safeguards.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code explicitly extracts PatientName, PatientID, dates, UIDs, and related DICOM fields, then prints or writes them. This is a clear privacy risk, although it matches the skill purpose.

medium_findings.title: Anonymization helper is incomplete for full DICOM de-identification
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE. The anonymization script removes a fixed list of top-level PHI fields, but UID anonymization is commented out and pixel data is preserved. DICOM files can also contain PHI in private tags, nested sequences, burned-in annotations, and institution-specific fields.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed code shows a finite tag list and commented UID regeneration. The remaining privacy gaps are common DICOM de-identification concerns and require warning rather than blocking.

low_findings.title: Documentation examples triggered false command-execution findings
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE. Static analysis flagged many Markdown code fences and inline backticks as Ruby or shell execution. The reviewed locations are documentation examples for pydicom usage, installation commands, or script invocation, not code that executes shell commands at runtime.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is Markdown documentation and Python snippets. No subprocess, os.system, eval, or runtime shell execution was found in the scripts.

low_findings.title: DICOM UIDs and compression names triggered false crypto and network findings
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE. Static analysis treated DICOM numeric UIDs, JPEG names, and transfer syntax references as weak cryptography or hardcoded IP addresses. These values identify medical image encoding formats and do not perform cryptographic operations or network access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The values are DICOM transfer syntax identifiers and pydicom constants. There is no hashing, encryption, socket use, or outbound request logic in these locations.

low_findings.title: Local output paths can overwrite user-selected files
low_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE. The helper scripts write anonymized DICOM files, converted images, or extracted metadata to paths supplied by the user. This is expected local filesystem behavior, but users should avoid untrusted paths and verify outputs before sharing them.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The scripts directly write to caller-provided output paths. This is normal CLI behavior, but it creates minor overwrite and data-placement risk.

content.value_statement: Medical imaging files contain complex metadata, pixel data, and transfer syntax details. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code inspect, convert, anonymize, and reason about DICOM files with pydicom.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to read DICOM files and access metadata with pydicom.
Shows how to extract pixel arrays and apply display windowing.
Provides scripts for anonymizing DICOM files, converting images, and exporting metadata.
Documents common DICOM tags, transfer syntaxes, and compression handlers.
Guides handling of multi-frame images, series sorting, and simple volume construction.
Covers practical troubleshooting for missing attributes and compressed pixel data.
limitations: The anonymization script is not a complete clinical de-identification workflow.
It does not validate diagnostic correctness or clinical image quality.
Compressed DICOM support depends on installed codecs such as pylibjpeg or gdcm.
Large studies can require substantial memory when pixel arrays are loaded.

use_cases.title: Prepare research image datasets
use_cases.description: Inspect DICOM metadata, convert selected images, and remove common PHI fields before internal research review.

use_cases.title: Debug radiology data pipelines
use_cases.description: Check transfer syntax, image dimensions, pixel spacing, and missing tags when a DICOM workflow fails.

use_cases.title: Learn pydicom workflows
use_cases.description: Use examples and references to understand reading, modifying, saving, and visualizing DICOM datasets.

prompt_templates.title: Inspect a DICOM file
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the pydicom skill to explain how I can inspect the patient, study, series, and image metadata in this DICOM file.

prompt_templates.title: Convert a scan to PNG
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the pydicom skill to convert a DICOM image to PNG and explain how windowing affects the output image.

prompt_templates.title: Review anonymization coverage
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the pydicom skill to review whether this anonymization approach removes common PHI fields and list remaining DICOM privacy risks.

prompt_templates.title: Design a series processing workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the pydicom skill to design a workflow that reads a DICOM series, sorts slices, checks spacing, and creates a 3D volume safely.

output_examples.input: A user asks why a DICOM file will not display after loading pixel data.
output_examples.output: The response identifies missing compression handlers as a likely cause, recommends checking the transfer syntax, and suggests installing the matching pixel decoder.

output_examples.input: A user asks whether an anonymized DICOM file is safe to share.
output: It warns about UIDs, private tags, nested sequences, and burned-in annotations.
It recommends a formal de-identification review before external sharing.

output_examples.input: A user asks how to export metadata for audit review.
output_examples.output: The response describes using the metadata extraction helper, choosing text or JSON output, and protecting files that may contain PHI.

best_practices: Work on copies of clinical DICOM files and keep originals unchanged.
Verify anonymization with a second review before sharing any medical data.
Check transfer syntax and installed codecs before loading large pixel arrays.
anti_patterns: Do not treat the helper anonymizer as full regulatory de-identification.
Do not publish extracted metadata without checking for PHI.
Do not load large or untrusted DICOM studies without resource limits.

faq.question: Can this skill read DICOM metadata?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains pydicom metadata access and includes a helper script that exports selected fields.

faq.question: Can it convert DICOM images to PNG or JPEG?
faq.answer: Yes. The conversion helper reads pixel data and saves common image formats such as PNG, JPEG, TIFF, and BMP.

faq.question: Does it fully anonymize medical images?
faq.answer: No. It removes common top-level PHI fields, but full DICOM de-identification requires deeper review.

faq.question: Does the skill send data to a network service?
faq.answer: No evidence found. The reviewed scripts operate on local files and documentation links are references only.

faq.question: Why are extra packages sometimes needed?
faq.answer: Some compressed DICOM transfer syntaxes require pixel data handlers such as pylibjpeg, pillow, or gdcm.

faq.question: Is this suitable for clinical decisions?
faq.answer: No. It is a development and data processing aid, not a diagnostic or clinical validation tool.
